title: Allyship and the Anger of Those You're Standing With
dek: *Standing beside someone in their fight means standing beside them in their fury. When you flinch at the anger of those you claim to support, you're asking them to shrink so you can feel safe. Real allyship isn't about comfort. It's about staying in the room when the temperature rises, when voices crack with decades of unspoken truth, and your own nervous system starts screaming for the exit.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. True allyship means staying present when the people you're standing with express anger, not just when they're palatable. Your discomfort with their rage often reveals how much you still expect them to manage your feelings. Learning to witness anger without rescuing, fixing, or shutting down is the work that separates performance from partnership.

You've been told that good allies stay calm. That steadiness, that measured tone, that ability to keep the peace while advocating for justice. But here's what nobody mentions: when you prioritize your own comfort over someone else's right to rage, you're not practicing allyship. You're practicing control. And the people you claim to stand with can feel the difference in their bones. ## The Performance vs. The Practice Allyship has become a badge people wear, a box to check, a public identity to claim. You post the right things. You show up to the marches. You read the books. And then someone you're ostensibly supporting gets angry in a way that makes your chest tight, and suddenly you're the one who needs soothing. In my own practice, I've watched this pattern repeat across every kind of marginalized experience. The white ally who can handle discussions of systemic racism until a Black colleague's voice rises. The straight supporter who champions LGBTQ+ rights until a queer friend snaps at yet another invasive question. Theabled advocate who freezes when a disabled person expresses fury at being infantilized again. What's happening in these moments isn't a failure of the angry person to communicate "properly." It's your nervous system detecting a threat to your self-image. Harriet Lerner's work on the dance of anger illuminates this beautifully: we're conditioned to see anger as dangerous, particularly when it comes from people we've been taught should be grateful for our support. The anger itself becomes evidence that you're doing something wrong, and your system mobilizes to shut it down. But anger isn't a referendum on your character. It's information. It's the accumulated weight of a thousand microaggressions, structural barriers, and exhausting explanations. When you make it about you, when you need reassurance that you're "one of the good ones," you're asking the marginalized person to perform emotional labor on top of everything else they're carrying. ## What Your Body Does When Anger Arrives Let's get specific about what happens in your system when someone you're supporting expresses rage. Your heart rate spikes. Your throat tightens. Your stomach drops. Maybe your face flushes hot or your hands go cold. the NIH overview of anger and the autonomic nervous system maps these responses clearly: your sympathetic nervous system reads raised voices, sharp gestures, or intense emotion as danger. Here's the crucial part: that physiological response isn't wrong. Your body is doing exactly what it's designed to do. But what you do next matters enormously. Do you recognize the sensation as your own nervous system activation and breathe through it? Or do you demand that the angry person calm down so you can feel safe again? Pat Ogden's somatic psychology work emphasizes that we can hold two truths simultaneously: your body's alarm is real, and it's not the responsibility of the angry person to deactivate it. You can notice your racing pulse, the urge to flee or appease, the tightness in your shoulders. You can even name it internally: "My system is activated right now." And then you can stay. This is body-anchored allyship. Your feet on the floor. Your breath moving in and out. The warmth or coolness of your palms. The pressure of the chair against your back. These sensations ground you in the present moment instead of the story your nervous system is telling about threat. When you practice staying present with your own activation instead of demanding someone else regulate it for you, you create space for their anger to exist without needing to be managed, softened, or justified. You stop being an audience requiring a performance and become a witness to lived experience. ## The Anger You Can Handle vs. The Anger You Can't There's a version of anger most self-identified allies can tolerate: the righteous, articulate, historically-grounded anger that sounds like a TED talk. The kind that comes with footnotes and measured pauses and doesn't actually make you feel implicated. Then there's the raw, unpolished, fuck-you anger that emerges when someone has explained the same thing seventeen times and you still don't get it. The anger that doesn't care about your learning curve. The anger that isn't interested in making you comfortable. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to this distinction: anger that centers the feelings of the person in power versus anger that centers the experience of the person harmed. When you can only handle the first kind, you're not practicing allyship. You're choosing your emotional experience of someone else's oppression. Soraya Chemaly's research on rage and women's anger reveals how deeply we're socialized to police emotional expression, particularly from people we view as "other." We expect anger to arrive with an instruction manual, to be educational rather than disruptive, to leave room for our fragility. But liberation doesn't wait for everyone to be ready. It doesn't pause to check if you're comfortable. The anger you can't handle is often the anger you most need to witness. It's the anger that's been swallowed for years to keep you at ease. It's the anger that's been translated, softened, and repackaged so many times it barely resembles the original feeling. When it finally emerges in its raw form, your discomfort is not a sign that something's wrong. It's a sign that something true is finally being spoken. ## The Practice: Five Minutes of Staying Here's something you can do right now, a practice for the next time you encounter anger from someone you're claiming to support: Set a timer for five minutes. During those five minutes, your only job is to stay present. Not to fix, not to defend, not to explain, not to make it better. Just to stay. Notice when your body wants to flee: the urge to check your phone, to interrupt with an apology, to redirect the conversation. Notice and don't act on it. Keep your feet planted. Keep breathing. Let the anger exist in the room without needing to do anything about it. When the timer goes off, you can respond. But first, practice the new act of simply bearing witness. This isn't about becoming a passive receptacle for abuse. It's about distinguishing between anger directed at systems you benefit from and personal attacks, and learning to stay present for the former without centering your discomfort. If you find your system regularly overwhelmed by this work, [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) includes gentle nervines that support your capacity to remain grounded without numbing out. ## When Your Comfort Becomes the Priority Watch what happens in advocacy spaces when someone's anger makes the room uncomfortable. Suddenly there's talk of "tone policing" as if naming tone policing means you can't ever discuss communication. There's concern about "alienating potential allies" as if the movement exists to recruit you rather than to achieve liberation. Your comfort becoming the priority is a red flag. It means the conversation has shifted from the actual issue to your feelings about how the issue is being presented. It means you're asking people who are already exhausted to add "managing the ally's emotions" to their unpaid labor. In our experience, this shift happens so smoothly that most people don't notice it occurring. One moment you're listening to someone describe their experience of discrimination. The next moment you're explaining why their anger makes it hard for you to hear them. The focus has moved from their reality to your reception of it. This is where [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) becomes essential. Fire element work helps you distinguish between anger that's yours to process and anger that's someone else's to express. When you can hold your own heat without needing to extinguish someone else's, you stop making their rage about your comfort. The question isn't whether someone's anger makes you uncomfortable. Of course it does. The question is whether you believe your comfort matters more than their truth. Whether you're willing to sit with the discomfort long enough to understand what's being communicated beneath the volume. ## The Difference Between Centering and Witnessing Centering yourself in someone else's anger looks like: "I feel attacked." "I'm trying my best." "Not all allies." "I didn't mean it that way." "Can we take a break so I can process this?" Witnessing someone else's anger looks like: silence. Presence. Breath. "I'm listening." "Tell me more." "I hear you." And then, after they've finished, after you've sat with it, after you've done your own work to process your activation: "Thank you for trusting me with this. What do you need from me?" The difference is where your attention goes. Centering means your feelings become the main event. Witnessing means you can hold your feelings without making them the point. This doesn't mean you don't matter. It means that in this specific moment, in this specific dynamic, your discomfort is not the emergency. The systems that generated this anger are the emergency. Your feelings about being confronted with those systems are real and valid and also not the priority right now. Sometimes people need support processing the intensity of witnessing someone else's rage. That's legitimate. But you get that support from your therapist, your journal, your peer group, or resources like those at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com). You don't get it from the person whose anger you just witnessed. They're not your emotional support system. They're the person you claimed to stand with. ## When Anger Reveals What You Actually Believe Here's what anger does: it shows you what you actually believe beneath what you say you believe. You can claim to support disabled rights until a disabled activist expresses fury at your inaccessible event, and then suddenly you're defensive about how hard you tried. You can post about believing survivors until a survivor's rage at their abuser makes you uncomfortable, and then you're worried about "both sides." Anger is diagnostic. It reveals where your allyship is performative versus practiced. Where you're willing to stand and where you need the people you're supporting to make it easier for you. Over the years I've seen people discover that their commitment to justice was actually a commitment to feeling like a good person. That their advocacy was contingent on being thanked for it. That their solidarity had a temperature limit, and when things got too hot, they needed everyone to calm down. These discoveries are painful. They require you to sit with the gap between your self-image and your actual behavior. But they're also necessary. Because real allyship isn't about your identity. It's about your actions, your capacity to stay, your willingness to be uncomfortable without making that discomfort everyone else's problem. [The assessments hub](/assessments) can help you track your own patterns around this. Where do you consistently struggle to stay present? What types of anger trigger your defensive responses? What stories does your nervous system tell about what anger means? This self-knowledge doesn't make you a better person. It makes you a more honest one, and honesty is the foundation of actual solidarity. ## The Overnight Regulation Question Sometimes at night, after a day of witnessing someone's justified rage, your system stays activated. Your mind replays the conversation. Your body holds the tension. This isn't unusual, especially if you're genuinely confronting your own complicity for the first time. If you find yourself struggling with sleep after these encounters, gentle support like [Gaia Herbs Sound Sleep](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0036THNRA?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help your nervous system downregulate without numbing you to the important emotional work. The goal isn't to erase the discomfort but to let your body rest while your psyche processes. For patterns of middle-of-the-night waking where you're mentally rehashing the interaction, [Bach Holly Flower Remedy (alt SKU)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005VW2I7K?tag=spankyspinola-20) addresses the specific emotional signature of feeling targeted or attacked, which can arise when your ego is confronting its own limitations. And for sustained support through longer periods of this work, [Gaia Herbs SleepThru (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004GJYTF8?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers consistent overnight nervous system regulation. The point isn't to sleep through your reckoning. It's to be rested enough to keep showing up for it. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### What if someone's anger actually is misdirected at me personally? Anger can be both justified in its root and imprecise in its target. Your job is still to stay present long enough to understand what's underneath it. Often what feels "misdirected" is actually you being confronted with your participation in a system you didn't think you were part of. That said, if someone is being abusive, you can set a boundary while still acknowledging the validity of their broader anger. The key is not using "this feels personal" as a way to dismiss the entire message. ### How do I know if I'm actually being a good ally or just performing? You probably won't know in the moment, and that uncertainty is part of the work. Performance seeks validation and reassurance. Practice focuses on impact over intention. Ask yourself: Am I doing this to be seen as good, or am I doing this because it's right? Am I staying when it's uncomfortable, or only when it feels rewarding? Do I need the people I'm supporting to thank me, or can I show up regardless? The answers will shift over time. Stay honest with yourself about where you actually are. ### Can I ever express my own feelings about someone's anger toward me? Yes, but timing and context matter enormously. In the immediate moment when someone is expressing rage about their experience, centering your feelings derails the conversation. Later, in a separate conversation, you can process your own emotional response. But that processing happens with your support system, not with the person whose anger you witnessed. They're not responsible for managing your reaction to their truth. --- The work of real allyship happens in the space between your nervous system screaming "threat" and your conscious choice to stay anyway. It happens when you notice your impulse to defend, to explain, to make it better, and you breathe through it instead of acting on it. It happens when you finally understand that someone else's anger isn't about making you comfortable. It never was. This doesn't mean you'll always get it right. You'll flinch. You'll center yourself. You'll ask someone to calm down when what you really mean is "please make this easier for me." The question is what you do after you notice. Do you double down and demand they manage your feelings? Or do you acknowledge the impulse, apologize if needed, and practice staying present next time? The path forward isn't paved with perfection. It's built from the willingness to keep showing up, to keep learning, to keep choosing presence over comfort even when every cell in your body wants to flee. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on allyship and the anger of those you're standing with, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
